g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Understanding Static in Java. We'll explore static variables, methods, and their key differences from instance members.
The static keyword in Java makes variables and methods belong to the class itself, not to individual objects created from that class.
g.text: The static keyword in Java is used to denote
a variable, method, or block that belongs to the
CLASS ITSELF, not to instances of the class.

g.data-narration: Static members are shared among all instances, loaded only once when the class loads, and can be accessed using the class name directly.
g.text: Shared among ALL instances of a class
Loaded once when class is loaded

g.data-narration: A static variable is declared using the static keyword. It's loaded into memory only once when the class is first loaded into the JVM.
Static variables are shared across all instances. When one instance modifies a static variable, all other instances see that change immediately.
Let's see a complete example. When we change Employee.company, both e1 and e2 display the new company name.
Understanding memory allocation is crucial. Static variables live in the method area and are shared, while instance variables live in heap memory per object.
Static methods belong to the class, not instances. They can be called without creating an object using ClassName.methodName syntax.
g.text: Static methods can be called WITHOUT creating an instance:

g.data-narration: Important restriction: static methods cannot directly access instance variables or instance methods because they don't have a 'this' reference.
Here's an example of a utility method that's perfect for static: a method that prints company info doesn't need instance data.
Let's compare static and instance members across key dimensions: memory, access, sharing behavior, and when to use each.
When a Java class loads, static variables are initialized first and only once. This happens before any object is created from the class.
Using static has implications: shared state across objects, memory efficiency but potential for bugs, and can't be overridden in subclasses.
Let's summarize what we've learned: static members belong to the class, are shared, and are loaded once. Use them wisely for shared state and utilities.
g.text: Static variables shared by ALL instances
Static methods don't need object creation
Loaded once in method area at class load time
Use for: counters, configs, utility methods

g.data-narration: For exams, remember: static means shared, one copy only, belongs to class. Practice identifying static variables in code and understanding their impact.
That's the end of this lecture on Understanding Static in Java. Thank you for watching, and good luck with your studies!
